subject: Physics
q: A tuning fork arrangement (pair) produces 4 beats / sec with one fork of frequency $288 \mathrm{cps}$. A little wax is placed on the unknown fork and it then produces 2 beats /sec. The frequency of the unknown fork is
question: A tuning fork arrangement (pair) produces 4 beats / sec with one fork of frequency $288 \mathrm{cps}$. A little wax is placed on the unknown fork and it then produces 2 beats /sec. The frequency of the unknown fork is
solution:
The fact that placing wax decreases the frequency of the unknown fork and also the beat frequency states that the unknown fork is of higher frequency.
$$
n-288=4 \Rightarrow n=292 \mathrm{cps}
$$

subject: Physics
q:
A person driving car at a constant speed of 15 m s-1 is approaching a vertical wall. The person notices a change of 40 Hz in the frequency of his car’s horn upon reflection from the wall. The frequency of horn is_______Hz.

(Given: Speed of sound: 30 m s-1)
question:
A person driving car at a constant speed of 15 m s-1 is approaching a vertical wall. The person notices a change of 40 Hz in the frequency of his car’s horn upon reflection from the wall. The frequency of horn is_______Hz.

(Given: Speed of sound: 30 m s-1)
solution:
The formula to calculate the frequency f' as heard by an observer with respect to the frequency f produced from a source is given by

f'= v±vov∓vsf   ....(1)

where, v is the speed of sound in air, vo is the speed of the observer and vs is the speed of the source.

In the first situation, the source is the moving car and the observer is the wall. Hence, the frequency fw as heard on the wall can be written as 

fw= v-0v-vcf= vv-vcf    ....(2)

where, vc is the speed of the car.

In the second situation, when the sound reflects back from the wall, the source of sound is the wall and the observer is the car.

Hence, the frequency fc of sound as heard by the car after the reflection is given by

fc= v+vcv-0fw= v+vcvfw    ....(3)

From equation (2) and (3), it follows that

fc= v+vcv×vv-vcf= v+vcv-vcf     ....(4)

It is given in the problem that

fc-f= 40    .....(5)

Substitute the expression from equation (4) into equation (5) and solve to calculate the frequency of the horn.

v+vcv-vcf-f=40⇒2vcv-vcf= 40

⇒f= 40v-vc2vc = 40×330-152×15 m s-1 = 420 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A closed and an open organ pipe have same lengths. If the ratio of frequencies of their seventh overtones is $\left(\frac{a-1}{a}\right)$ then the value of $\mathrm{a}$ is ________
question: A closed and an open organ pipe have same lengths. If the ratio of frequencies of their seventh overtones is $\left(\frac{a-1}{a}\right)$ then the value of $\mathrm{a}$ is ________
solution:
For closed organ pipe
$\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}=(2 \mathrm{n}+1) \frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \ell}=\frac{15 \mathrm{v}}{4 \ell}$
For open organ pipe
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{o}}=(\mathrm{n}+1) \frac{\mathrm{v}}{2 \ell}=\frac{8 \mathrm{v}}{2 \ell} \\ & \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{f}_{\mathrm{o}}}=\frac{15}{16}=\frac{\mathrm{a}-1}{\mathrm{a}} \\ & \Rightarrow \mathrm{a}=16\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A closed organ pipe 150 cm long gives 7 beats per second with an open organ pipe of length 350 cm, both vibrating in fundamental mode. The velocity of sound is ______ m s-1.
question: A closed organ pipe 150 cm long gives 7 beats per second with an open organ pipe of length 350 cm, both vibrating in fundamental mode. The velocity of sound is ______ m s-1.
solution:
The formula to calculate the fundamental frequency in a closed pipe is given by

fc=v4l1   ...1

And, that in an open pipe is given by

fo=v2l2   ...2

Given that

fc-fo=7   ...3

From equations (1), (2) and (3), it follows that

v4×150-v2×350=7⇒v600 cm-v700 cm=7⇒v6m-v7m=7⇒v142=7⇒v=42×7=294 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: Two factories are sounding their sirens at 800 $\mathrm{Hz}$. A man goes from one factory to other at a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity of sound is $320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard by the person in one second will be:
question: Two factories are sounding their sirens at 800 $\mathrm{Hz}$. A man goes from one factory to other at a speed of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity of sound is $320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The number of beats heard by the person in one second will be:
solution:
Given: Frequency of sound produced by siren, $f=800 \mathrm{~Hz}$
Speed of observer, $u=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Velocity of sound, $v=320 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
No. of beats heard per second $=$ ?
No. of extra waves received by the observer per second $=\pm 4 \lambda$
$\therefore \quad$ No. of beats/ sec
$$
\begin{aligned}
&=\frac{2}{\lambda}-\left(-\frac{2}{\lambda}\right)=\frac{4}{\lambda} \\
&=\frac{2 \times 2}{\frac{320}{800}} \quad\left(\because \lambda=\frac{V}{f}\right) \\
&=\frac{2 \times 2 \times 800}{320}=10
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A resonance tube is old and has a jagged end. It is still used in the laboratory to determine the velocity of sound in air. A tuning fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>512</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> produces first resonance when the tube is filled with water to a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below a reference mark, near the open end of the tube. The experiment is repeated with another fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>256</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> which produces first resonance when water reaches a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below the reference mark. The velocity of sound in air, obtained in the experiment, is close to
question: A resonance tube is old and has a jagged end. It is still used in the laboratory to determine the velocity of sound in air. A tuning fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>512</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> produces first resonance when the tube is filled with water to a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>11</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below a reference mark, near the open end of the tube. The experiment is repeated with another fork of frequency <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>256</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>Hz</mi></math> which produces first resonance when water reaches a mark <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>cm</mi></math> below the reference mark. The velocity of sound in air, obtained in the experiment, is close to
solution:
λ14=11+e ⇒v512 × 4=11+e    ...(1)

λ24=27+e ⇒v256 × 4=27+e    ...(2)

Subtracting the equations,

v256×4×12=16 cm=0.16 m

⇒v=0.16×2×4×256

         =328 m s-1

subject: Physics
q: A wave travelling along the $x$-axis is described by the equation $y(x, t)=0.005 \cos (\alpha x-\beta t)$. If the wavelength and the time period of the wave are $0.08 \mathrm{~m}$ and $2.0 \mathrm{~s}$, respectively, then $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in appropriate units are
question: A wave travelling along the $x$-axis is described by the equation $y(x, t)=0.005 \cos (\alpha x-\beta t)$. If the wavelength and the time period of the wave are $0.08 \mathrm{~m}$ and $2.0 \mathrm{~s}$, respectively, then $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in appropriate units are
solution:
$$
y=0.005 \cos (\alpha x-\beta \mathrm{t})
$$
comparing the equation with the standard form,
$$
\begin{aligned}
& y=A \cos \left[\left(\frac{\mathrm{x}}{\lambda}-\frac{\mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{T}}\right) 2 \pi\right] \\
& 2 \pi / \lambda=\alpha \text { and } 2 \pi / T=\beta \\
& \alpha=2 \pi / 0.08=25.00 \pi \\
& \beta=\pi
\end{aligned}
$$

solution:
wave velocity=nλ

(Vp)max=aω

nλ=2a2πn

λ=2(a)2π

=2.10π.2π

= 40 cm

solution:
Frequency appeared at wall

fw=330330-v.f    ......(1)

question: Two travelling waves produces a standing wave represented by equation. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mi>cos</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>57</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mi>x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced close="]" open="["><mrow><mfenced><mrow><mn>78</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>. The node closest to the origin in the region <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></math> will be at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo>.</mo><mo> </mo><mfenced><mrow><mtext>in</mtext><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
At Node,
y=0

⇒cos1.57xsin78.5t=0

⇒cos(1.57x)=0

⇒1.57x=π2

⇒x=1 cm

subject: Physics
q:
A transverse wave on a string is described by $y = 3\sin(36t + 0.018x + \pi/4)$, where $x, y$ are in cm and $t$ in seconds. The least distance between the two successive crests in the wave is _____ cm. (Nearest integer)
($\pi = 3.14$)
question:
A transverse wave on a string is described by $y = 3\sin(36t + 0.018x + \pi/4)$, where $x, y$ are in cm and $t$ in seconds. The least distance between the two successive crests in the wave is _____ cm. (Nearest integer)
($\pi = 3.14$)
solution:
The standard equation of a transverse wave is given by $y = A\sin(\omega t + kx + \phi)$

Comparing this with the given equation $y = 3\sin(36t + 0.018x + \pi/4)$, we get the wave number $k = 0.018 \text{ cm}^{-1}$.

The least distance between two successive crests is the wavelength $\lambda$ of the wave.

We know that $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{k} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.14}{0.018}$

$\lambda = \dfrac{6.28}{0.018} = \dfrac{6280}{18} = 348.88 \text{ cm}$

Rounding to the nearest integer, we get $349 \text{ cm}$.

Answer: $349$

subject: Physics
q: A closed organ pipe has a fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kHz</mi></math>. The number of overtones that can be distinctly heard by a person with this organ pipe will be <math><mo>(</mo></math>Assume that the highest frequency a person can hear is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20,000</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi></math>).
question: A closed organ pipe has a fundamental frequency of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1.5</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>kHz</mi></math>. The number of overtones that can be distinctly heard by a person with this organ pipe will be <math><mo>(</mo></math>Assume that the highest frequency a person can hear is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20,000</mn><mtext> </mtext><mi>Hz</mi></math>).
solution:
For a closed organ pipe, the resonate frequency(harmonics) is an odd multiple of the fundamental frequency.

Therefore, 2n-1f0≤20000 Hz, where n=1,2,3...

f0=1.5 kHz⇒ fundamental frequency

Then, n=7

The total number of overtones that can be heard is 6. (2nd harmonic to 7th harmonic. )

subject: Physics
q: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency?
question: An observer moves towards a stationary source of sound, with a velocity one fifth of the velocity of sound. What is the percentage increase in the apparent frequency?
solution:
$$
f=\frac{v+v / 5}{v} f=\frac{6 f}{5}
$$
$\%$ increase in frequency $=20 \%$

660330+u0330-0-330-u0330-0=10
22u0=10
u0=2.5 m/s

subject: Physics
q: A radar sends an electromagnetic signal of electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> which strikes a target on line of sight at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> in a medium. After that, a part of signal (echo) reflects back towards the radar with same velocity and by same path. If the signal was transmitted at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> from radar, then after how much time echo will reach to the radar?
question: A radar sends an electromagnetic signal of electric field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>E</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>25</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">V</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and magnetic field <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><msub><mi>B</mi><mn>0</mn></msub></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> which strikes a target on line of sight at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo> </mo><mi>km</mi></math> in a medium. After that, a part of signal (echo) reflects back towards the radar with same velocity and by same path. If the signal was transmitted at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> from radar, then after how much time echo will reach to the radar?
solution:
The speed of light is given by,

c=E0 B0=2.251.5×10-8=1.5×108 m s-1

Therefore, time taken for the echo will be,

t=2dc=6×1031.5×108=4×10-5 s

question:
A closed organ pipe of length L and an open organ pipe contain gases of densities ρ1 and ρ2 respectively. The compressibility of gases are equal in both the pipes. Both the pipes are vibrating in their first overtone with same frequency. The length of the open pipe is x3Lρ1ρ2, where x is _______.

solution:
fc=f0

3VC4L=2V02L'

3VC4L=V0L'

L'=4L3V0VC=4L3B.ρ1ρ2.B   (B is bulk modulus)

=4L3ρ1ρ2

x=4

subject: Physics
q: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
question: Two harmonic waves moving in the same direction superimpose to form a wave $\mathrm{x}=\mathrm{a} \cos (1.5 \mathrm{t}) \cos (50.5 \mathrm{t})$ where $t$ is in seconds. Find the period with which they beat (close to nearest integer)
solution:
The given equation can be written as
$\mathrm{x}=\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [1.5+50.5] \mathrm{t}+\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2} \cos [50.5-1.5]$
$x=\frac{a}{2} \cos [52 t]+\frac{a}{2} \cos [49 t]$
Here, $2 \pi \mathrm{f}_1 \& 2 \pi \mathrm{f}_2=49$
$\mathrm{f}_1=\frac{52}{2 \pi}, \mathrm{f}_2=\frac{49}{2 \pi}$
$\therefore \mathrm{f}_{\text {Bat }}=\mathrm{f}_1-\mathrm{f}_2=\frac{3}{2 \pi} \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\therefore \mathrm{T}_{\text {Beat }}=\frac{1}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {Beat }}}=\frac{2 \pi}{3} \mathrm{sec}$
$=2.09 \mathrm{sec} \approx 2 \mathrm{sec}$

subject: Physics
q: In the resonance experiment, two air columns (closed at one end) of 100 cm and 120 cm long, give 15 beats per second when each one is sounding in the respective fundamental modes. The velocity of sound in the air column is :
question: In the resonance experiment, two air columns (closed at one end) of 100 cm and 120 cm long, give 15 beats per second when each one is sounding in the respective fundamental modes. The velocity of sound in the air column is :
solution:
Fundamental frequency in close/organ pipe
$\begin{aligned} & (\mathrm{f})=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \ell} \\ & \mathrm{f}_1=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \ell_1} \& \mathrm{f}_2=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4 \ell_2}\end{aligned}$
Beat $=\left(\mathrm{f}_1-\mathrm{f}_2\right)=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4}\left(\frac{1}{\ell_1}-\frac{1}{\ell_2}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} & 15=\frac{\mathrm{v}}{4}\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1.2}\right) \\ & \mathrm{v}=\left(\frac{15 \times 4 \times 1.2}{0.2}\right)=60 \times 6=360 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A uniform thin rope of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hangs vertically from a rigid support and a block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to its free end. A transverse short wave train of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is produced at the lower and the rope. What is the wavelength of the wave train (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> ) when it reaches the top of the rope?
question: A uniform thin rope of length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> and mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> hangs vertically from a rigid support and a block of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is attached to its free end. A transverse short wave train of wavelength <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is produced at the lower and the rope. What is the wavelength of the wave train (in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> ) when it reaches the top of the rope?
solution:
V=fλ

V1λ1=V2λ2

λ2=V2V1λ1=T2T1λ1T2=8gTop

8g2gλ1

T1=2gBottom=2λ1=12cm

subject: Physics
q: A driver in a car, approaching a vertical wall notices that the frequency of his car horn has changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>440</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>480</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, when it gets reflected from the wall. If the speed of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>345</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the speed of the car is:
question: A driver in a car, approaching a vertical wall notices that the frequency of his car horn has changed from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>440</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>480</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Hz</mi></math>, when it gets reflected from the wall. If the speed of sound in air is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>345</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>, then the speed of the car is:
solution:
Given,

Frequency of sound after reflection from the wall is 

f''=480 Hz

Frequency of sound emitted by the horn of car is 

f=440 Hz

Speed of sound is v=345 m s-1

The diver in the car (as a source) approaching the vertical wall which is the observer in this case, The frequency of sound received by the stationary wall is given by

fr'=vv-vcf  ...(1)

As the frequency f' is received by wall will act as a source now, and driver in the car is an observer, the frequency of sound received by driver is ,

fr''=v+vcvf'  ...(2)

Equating equation (1) and (2) we get,

fr''=v+vcv-vcf  ...(3)

Substitute the given value in the equation (3), we get
⇒480=345+vc345-vc440

⇒1211=345+vc345-vc

Velocity of car is given by

⇒vc=54 km hr-1

subject: Physics
q: The speed of sound in oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ at a certain temperature is $460 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The speed of sound in helium (He) at the same temperature will be (assumed both gases to be ideal)
question: The speed of sound in oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)$ at a certain temperature is $460 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. The speed of sound in helium (He) at the same temperature will be (assumed both gases to be ideal)
solution:
$$
\begin{aligned}
& v=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}} \\
& \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma_1 M_2}{\gamma_2 M_1}}=\sqrt{\frac{\frac{7}{5} \times 4}{\frac{5}{3} \times 32}} \\
& \frac{460}{V_2}=\sqrt{\frac{21}{25 \times 8}} \Rightarrow \quad v_2=\frac{460 \times 5 \times 2 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{21}}=1420
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Physics
q: A wave represented by the equation $y_1=a \cos$ $(k x-\omega t)$ is superimposed with another wave to form a stationary wave such that the point $x-0$ is node. The equation for the other wave is
question: A wave represented by the equation $y_1=a \cos$ $(k x-\omega t)$ is superimposed with another wave to form a stationary wave such that the point $x-0$ is node. The equation for the other wave is
solution:
Since the point $x=0$ is a node and reflection is taking place from point $x=0$. This means that reflection must be taking place from the fixed end and hence the reflected ray must suffer an additional phase change of $\pi$ or a path change of $\frac{\lambda}{2}$.
So, if $y_{\text {incident }}=a \cos (k x-\omega t)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow y_{\text {incident }} & =a \cos (-k x-\omega t+\pi) \\
& =-a \cos (\omega t+k x)
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence equation for the other wave
$$
y=a \cos (k x+\omega t+\pi)
$$

subject: Physics
q: A sonometer wire of length $114 \mathrm{~cm}$ is fixed at both the ends. Where should the two bridges be placed so as to divide the wire into three segments whose fundamental frequencies are in the ratio $1: 3: 4$ ?
question: A sonometer wire of length $114 \mathrm{~cm}$ is fixed at both the ends. Where should the two bridges be placed so as to divide the wire into three segments whose fundamental frequencies are in the ratio $1: 3: 4$ ?
solution:
Total length of the wire, $\mathrm{L}=114 \mathrm{~cm}$ $\mathrm{n}_1: \mathrm{n}_2: \mathrm{n}_3=1: 3: 4$
Let $\mathrm{L}_1, \mathrm{~L}_2$ and $\mathrm{L}_3$ be the lengths of the three parts
As $\mathrm{n} \propto \frac{1}{\mathrm{~L}}$
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{L}_1: \mathrm{L}_2: \mathrm{L}_3=\frac{1}{1}: \frac{1}{3}: \frac{1}{4}=12: 4: 3 \\
& \therefore \quad \mathrm{L}_1=72 \mathrm{~cm}\left(\frac{12}{12+4+3} \times 114\right) \\
& \mathrm{L}_2=24 \mathrm{~cm}\left(\frac{4}{19} \times 114\right) \\
& \text { and } \quad \mathrm{L}_3=18 \mathrm{~cm}\left(\frac{3}{19} \times 114\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Hence the bridges should be placed at $72 \mathrm{~cm}$ and $72+24=96 \mathrm{~cm}$ from one end.

subject: Physics
q: Two strings $(A, B)$ having linear densities $\mu_{A}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and, $\mu_{B}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and lengths $L_{A}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $L_{B}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are joined. Free ends of $A$ and $B$ are tied to two rigid supports $C$ and $D$, respectively creating a tension of 500 N in the wire. Two identical pulses, sent from $C$ and $D$ ends, take time $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$, respectively, to reach the joint. The ratio $t_{1} / t_{2}$ is :
question: Two strings $(A, B)$ having linear densities $\mu_{A}=2 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and, $\mu_{B}=4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}$ and lengths $L_{A}=2.5 \mathrm{~m}$ and $L_{B}=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$ respectively are joined. Free ends of $A$ and $B$ are tied to two rigid supports $C$ and $D$, respectively creating a tension of 500 N in the wire. Two identical pulses, sent from $C$ and $D$ ends, take time $t_{1}$ and $t_{2}$, respectively, to reach the joint. The ratio $t_{1} / t_{2}$ is :
solution:
Wave velocity in string: $v = \sqrt{\frac{T}{\mu}}$
Time to reach joint: $t = \frac{L}{v} = L\sqrt{\frac{\mu}{T}}$
$t_1 = L_A\sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{T}}$ and $t_2 = L_B\sqrt{\frac{\mu_B}{T}}$
$\frac{t_1}{t_2} = \frac{L_A}{L_B} \times \sqrt{\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}} = \frac{2.5}{1.5} \times \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 10^{-4}}}$
$= \frac{5}{3} \times \sqrt{\frac{1}{2}} = \frac{5}{3\sqrt{2}} = \frac{5\sqrt{2}}{6} \approx 1.18$

subject: Physics
q: A point source is kept at the center of a spherically enclosed detector. If the volume of the detector increased by 8 times, the intensity will
question: A point source is kept at the center of a spherically enclosed detector. If the volume of the detector increased by 8 times, the intensity will
solution:
For a point source, intensity is given by $I = \frac{P}{4\pi r^2}$ where $P$ is power and $r$ is distance.
If the volume of the spherical detector increases by 8 times, then $\frac{4}{3}\pi r_{new}^3 = 8 \cdot \frac{4}{3}\pi r_{old}^3$, giving $r_{new} = 2r_{old}$.
The new intensity is $I_{new} = \frac{P}{4\pi (2r_{old})^2} = \frac{1}{4} \cdot \frac{P}{4\pi r_{old}^2} = \frac{I_{old}}{4}$.
Therefore intensity decreases by 4 times.
